wylie: su ru phan tsa, sur phan
english: Guinea pepper, Capsicum annuum C., W.

sú-lui tsúl-du bżúgs-pa (su lu'i tshul du bzhugs pa) denotes perh. the usual sitting posture of Milaraspa who, while reciting his songs, used to stretch out his left leg, drawing up the other, and supporting his right arm on it on which his head was leaning.
1. v. su (su). -- 2. su-sú zer-wa (su su zer wa) denotes the drawing in the breath in blowing up a fire, the lips being nearly closed, to prevent ashes or smoke from entering the mouth.
reward, recompense, sug-rṅán (sug rngan) id., sug-rǰéd (sug rjed) mark of honour as a reward Cs.; pág-sug (phag sug) a bribe, frq.

I. sbst. 1. the hand Med.; sug-brís (sug bris) handwriting Sch. -- 2. the lower part of the leg of animals, rtai súg-bżi (rta'i sug bzhi); *súg-gu* (*sug gu*) W. id. -- 3. a medicinal herb Wdn. II. vb. to push, jog, nudge a person, in order to waken him, or to make him attentive; to push open, a door with a stick Mil.; súg-pa żíg byéd-pa (sug pa zhig byed pa) Mil., *sug gyáb-pa* (*sug rgyab pa*) C. to push, to shove, to displace, *sug ča yin* (*sug cha yin*) it will get out of its place W.
Cs.: 'a kind of spice, betel, betel-nut'(?); sug-smél (sug smel) Lt. and C. cardamom.
to cough, to breathe with difficulty Cs., súd-de śí-ba (sud de shi ba) to be choked or suffocated Sch.

vb. and adj. 1. to be tired of, weary of, sick of; tired, weary, out of humour rná-ba mi sún-te (rna ba mi sun te) not tired of hearing Mil.; yid sún-par gyúr-ba (yid sun par gyur ba) to become tired, to get weary of; the more precise form of the adj. is sún-po (sun po): *żág-daṅ ḍe zá-na sún-po rag* (*zhag dang 'bras za na sun po rag*) or *sún-na rag* (*sun na rag*) W. if one eats rice every day, one gets tired of it; *sems sun-po* (*sems sun po*) id.; *'ám-čog sụn soṅ* (*a mchog sun song*) C. one gets tired of hearing C.; *sá-heb nyeṅ sụn ruṅ* (*sa heb nyan sun rung*) though it may be disagreeable to you, Sir, to hear it C.; tugs-sún dgóṅs-pa yín-pa ḍug (thugs sun dgongs pa yin pa 'dug) I suppose your Reverence will be tired of it Mil.; *sun* (*sun*) (C. *sụn* (*sun*)) *ćug-će* (*bcug ce*) to make (a person) tired of (a thing), to vex, annoy. to stun or drown with noise, to deafen. -- 2. sún-par byéd-pa (sun par byed pa) Dzl., and more frq. sún-par byín-pa (sun par byin pa) 1. to drown with, to overpower by noise, to silence (thus prob. Mil. ch. 34 init.); hence 2. to refute, confute, disprove Tar. 3. c. acc. to insult, defame, disgrace, dishonour Schr., Dzl. ???, 1, 3, 29v, 2; Bhar. 67, Schf.; so perh. also sun-pa (sun pa) in the following passage of Mil.: ṭúgs-rǰe drág-po sun ma byin (thugs rje drag po sun ma byin) we will not put to shame the great favour (of the Lama). 4. to renounce, to resign, sun byín-pai stobs (sun byin pa'i stobs) strength to renounce (the world).

pf. (b)subs ((b)subs), fut. bsub (bsub), 1. to stop up, plug up, close, cork; to keep shut, closed, locked up, to stop, ka sna lág-pas (kha sna lag pas) to stop one's mouth and nose with one's hand Lt.; dbugs súb-pa (dbugs sub pa) to strangle, suffocate, choke (a person); to fill up, choke up (with earth, rubbish etc.) a lake Glr.; sna-sub (sna sub) a disease of the nose? Lt. -- 2. to cover, close, shut up Sch., more frq., fig. rkaṅ-rǰés súb-pa (rkang rjes sub pa) to cover the trace or track, to efface every vestige; *tí-pi ṭúr-la súb-će* (*ti pi thur la sub ce*) to turn down the brim of the hat; to blot out, erase, rí-mo (ri mo) a drawing, bú-lon-pai míṅ (bu lon pa'i ming) the name of a debtor; to hush up, conceal, cover, e.g. other people's offences; to suppress, to avoid, e.g. obscene words; to allow to settle, the mash, in brewing; in all these instances in W. also *súb-te bór-će* (*sub te bor ce*) is used.

for ysum (gsum), three, in compounds before consonants: súm-ču (sum cu) 30, sum-brgya (sum brgya) 300, sum-stóṅ (sum stong) 3000; súm-ča (sum cha), Sch. also sum-yaṛ (sum yar), a third, the third part, ḍzam-bu-glíṅ súm-ča ynyis ('dzam bu gling sum cha gnyis) (or only sum-ynyis (sum gnyis)) two thirds of Dzambuling (i.e. of the world) Dz., bod sum ynyis (bod sum gnyis) two thirds of (all) the Tibetans Ma.; sum-skyá (sum skya) Sch. a cord of three twisted threads; sum-ču-rtsa-ysum (sum cu rtsa gsum) the 33 ancient gods (of the Vedas); súm-ču-pa (sum cu pa) the thirty, i.e. 30 letters, the Tibetan alphabet, súm-ču-pa daṅ rtags-ʾjúg (sum cu pa dang rtags 'jug) Zam. the alphabet and the punctuation, abbreviated: sum-rtágs (sum rtags) Lexx.
